paraphrase: Aemon Targaryen, Benerro (the High Red Priest of Volantis), and Marwyn the Mage (who says he doesn't trust prophecy) all independently cite the same star/salt/smoke signs.
prior_failed_quote: Benerro has sent forth the word from Volantis. Her coming is the fulfillment of an ancient prophecy. From smoke and salt was she born to make the world anew. She is Azor Ahai returned…

paraphrase: At the same ceremony, Stannis draws a sword from the fire and names it Lightbringer.
prior_failed_quote: Behold Lightbringer!" and the ceremony context shows Stannis drawing the sword from the fire at the ceremony on Dragonstone.

paraphrase: Aemon says 'the sword is wrong,' and describes its light as an 'empty glamor' -- perhaps whatever magic it has is just another of Melisandre's illusions.
prior_note: The phrase 'the sword is wrong' appears at AFFC Sam IV:33. A fuller context would show Aemon's skepticism about the sword's authenticity.

spoken_quote: Only death can pay for life
paraphrase: Dany herself says, in the same chapter, 'Only death can pay for life,' tying these deaths to the birth of her dragons.
prior_failed_quote: Only death can pay for life.
prior_note: Dany states this principle in the same chapter as dragon hatching and the Bleeding Star, echoing Mirri Maz Duur's teaching.

paraphrase: Rhaegar Targaryen also had the blood and was born amid salt and smoke.
prior_failed_quote: Rhaegar was born at the Trident during war and turmoil
prior_note: Rhaegar born amid smoke and salt (tears of war), candidate for prophecy signs; ungrounded pending exact cite.

paraphrase: Victarion has a burned, smoking hand.
prior_failed_quote: Victarion Greyjoy has a burned hand
prior_note: Victarion's hand is burned and smoking; mentioned as a minor Azor Ahai candidate.

paraphrase: The Lightning Lord (Beric Dondarrion) has a burning sword.

prior_note: Beric's sword has burning/flaming quality; precedent for magical swords.

paraphrase: Ramsay shares the surname 'Snow' with Jon.
prior_note: Ramsay Bolton uses the bastard name 'Snow' like Jon; minor incidental similarity.

paraphrase: The Targaryens have historically been guided by visions and prophecy; they survived the Doom of Valyria because of visions of Rhaegar's ancestor Daenys the Dreamer.
prior_failed_quote: Targaryen prophecy tradition; they survived Doom of Valyria due to Daenys the Dreamer's visions

prior_note: Dunk & Egg novella references (Daeron, Daemon Blackfyre have prophetic dreams)

paraphrase: The three-headed dragon on the Targaryen banner represents the three siblings who led House Targaryen three hundred years ago -- Aegon, Rhaenys, and Visenya; Daenerys notes these names connect to Rhaegar's own children, Aegon and Rhaenys.
prior_failed_quote: Three-headed dragon sigil represents Aegon, Rhaenys, and Visenya (Fire & Blood history)
prior_note: Fire & Blood confirms three-headed dragon represents the three siblings; names match Rhaegar's children

paraphrase: Part of the Azor Ahai prophecy is that the hero will wield a 'burning sword' called Lightbringer.
prior_failed_quote: A sword plucked from fire, yes... A burning sword... A sword of flames, the red priestess had called it. Lightbringer.
prior_note: Lightbringer described as burning/flaming sword in Azor Ahai prophecy

paraphrase: In Book 4, Euron says that as a boy he dreamed he could fly, that if he just tried, if he leapt from a tower, he might fly -- which is just what happens to Bran when Brynden visits his dream and tells him to try to fly.
prior_failed_quote: When I was a boy, I dreamt that I could fly, he announced. When I woke, I couldn't ... or so the maester said. But what if he lied?

paraphrase: Maybe Brynden only wants Bran because his old body is dying and he plans to steal Bran's young body -- magical bodysnatching is established as a real thing in Book 5.
prior_failed_quote: A man could own no lands, the shogun had explained to him when he took the shadowbinder as his bride, no castles, no towers, no house, no throne. The shadowbinder was a free creature, beholden to none of the high lords of the Six Kingdoms.
prior_note: Varamyr Sixskins chapter discusses bodysnatching/possession of others; establishes magical precedent for B51 but no direct Brynden quote

spoken_quote: what is the life of one
paraphrase: As a thematic counterpoint, when Stannis considers killing a child to win the realm, he asks 'what is the life of one ... boy against a kingdom?' and Davos replies 'Everything' -- suggesting sacrificing basic humanity and compassion undermines the very thing being saved.
prior_failed_quote: If Joffrey should die ... what is the life of one bastard boy against a kingdom?

paraphrase: The identity question matters because Meera keeps pressing Coldhands about who he is, and he refuses to show his face or give his real name.
prior_failed_quote: Meera Reed rose, her frog spear in her hand, a chunk of smoking meat still impaled upon its tines. "Show us your face." The ranger made no move to obey. "He's dead."
prior_note: Meera and Bran directly confront Coldhands about his identity and demand to see his face, which he refuses

paraphrase: Other possible identities include Will and Waymar Royce, Night's Watchmen killed in the Prologue of A Game of Thrones.
prior_failed_quote: Will rose. Ser Waymar Royce stood over him. His fine clothes were a tatter, his face a ruin. A shard from his sword transfixed the blind white pupil of his left eye. The right eye was open. The pupil burned blue. It saw.
prior_note: Waymar Royce is killed by the Others and turns into a wight with blue-burning eyes; book shows he became a wight, not an independent entity

paraphrase: Old Nan says the man in the story may have been a Bolton, tying House Bolton directly to a union with an Other-like being.
prior_failed_quote: After his fall, when it was found he had been sacrificing to the Others, all records of Night's King had been destroyed, his very name forbidden. "Some say he was a Bolton," Old Nan would always end.
prior_note: Old Nan directly suggests Night's King might have been a Bolton, explicitly connecting House Bolton to the supernatural/Other-adjacent narrative.

paraphrase: Against the Night's King theory: he was reportedly cast down by the wildlings and the Starks, while Coldhands was reportedly killed by the Others -- and it's unclear why the Others would kill someone who had been making sacrifices to them, or why the wildlings and Starks would have let him live given they supposedly obliterated his very name from memory.
prior_note: S214 spend-wall gap: agent died before emitting this row

paraphrase: Against the hybrid theory: Coldhands doesn't seem part-Other -- Others are described as strange, beautiful creatures, a different sort of life, whereas Coldhands is just a dead man with no blue eyes, icy sword, or other White Walker features.
